coredata.dc:title: Molecular simulation of Cu, Ag, and Au-decorated Si-doped graphene quantum dots (Si@QD) nanostructured as sensors for SO2 trapping
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling
coredata.dc:creator: Louis, Hitler
Chukwuemeka, Kelechi
Agwamba, Ernest C.
Abdullah, Hewa Y.
Pembere, Anthony M.S.
coredata.dc:description: In view of the numerous environmental hazards and health challenges linked to sulfur (iv) oxide (SO2), an indirect greenhouse gas, and the resultant need to develop efficient gas nanosensor devices, this research had as its principal focus on the theoretical evaluation of the gas sensing potential of metals: Ag, Au and Cu functionalized silicon-doped quantum dots (Si@QD) for the detection and adsorption of SO2 gas investigated using the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) computation at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory. Eight (8) possible adsorption modes: SO2_O_Si@QD, SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_O_Au_Si@QD, SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD, SO2_S_Si@QD, SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_S_Au_Si@QD, and SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD were considered based on SO2 interactions with the studied materials at the -S and -O sites of the SO2 molecule. The counterpoise correction (BSSE) showed that five of the eight interactions had favorable Ead + BSSE values ranging from −0.31 to −1.98 eV. All the eight interactions were observed to be thermodynamically favorable with ΔG and ΔH ranging from −129.01 to −200.24 kcal/mol and −158.26 to −229.73 kcal/mol respectively. Results from the topology analysis reveal that van der Waals forces occurred the greatest at the gas-sensor interphase while SO2_S_ Cu_Si@QD is predicted to have the highest sensing potency based on the conductivity and recovery time estimations. These results confirm the potential efficient feasibility of real-world device application of the metals (Ag, Au, Cu) functionalized Si-doped QDs.

The optimized structures of the four S site interactions namely, SO2_S_Si@QD (C5), SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD (C6), SO2_S_Au_Si@QD (C7) and SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD (C8).

Dipole moment (debye, D) (along the three coordinate axes (X, Y, Z) of the Si-doped QD (Si@QD), the three (3) metal functionalized Si@QD (Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD), SO2 and the eight adsorbent-adsorbate complexes under consideration calculated at the DFT B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory.

the thermodynamic properties of the eight adsorbate-adsorbent systems computed at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP Level of theory where ΔH (eV, kcal/mol), ΔG (eV, Kcal/mol), E (eV, kcal/mol), Cp (cal/mol K−1) and S (cal/molK−1) denote enthalpy change, change in Gibb's free energy, thermal energy, heat capacity and entropy respectively.
QTAIM (Quantum theory of atoms in molecules analysis of the eight studied adsorbent-adsorbate interactions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the numerous environmental hazards and health challenges linked to sulfur (iv) oxide (SO2), an indirect greenhouse gas, and the resultant need to develop efficient gas nanosensor devices, this research had as its principal focus on the theoretical evaluation of the gas sensing potential of metals: Ag, Au and Cu functionalized silicon-doped quantum dots (Si@QD) for the detection and adsorption of SO2 gas investigated using the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) computation at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory. Eight (8) possible adsorption modes: SO2_O_Si@QD, SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_O_Au_Si@QD, SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD, SO2_S_Si@QD, SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_S_Au_Si@QD, and SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD were considered based on SO2 interactions with the studied materials at the -S and -O sites of the SO2 molecule. The counterpoise correction (BSSE) showed that five of the eight interactions had favorable Ead + BSSE values ranging from −0.31 to −1.98 eV. All the eight interactions were observed to be thermodynamically favorable with ΔG and ΔH ranging from −129.01 to −200.24 kcal/mol and −158.26 to −229.73 kcal/mol respectively. Results from the topology analysis reveal that van der Waals forces occurred the greatest at the gas-sensor interphase while SO2_S_ Cu_Si@QD is predicted to have the highest sensing potency based on the conductivity and recovery time estimations. These results confirm the potential efficient feasibility of real-world device application of the metals (Ag, Au, Cu) functionalized Si-doped QDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The quest to develop efficient systems for gas sensing has inspired numerous research expeditions, scientific conferences, publications as well as technological innovations in view of the all-encompassing catastrophic impacts of gases which in terms of hazardousness can be classified into different groups such as corrosive (e.g. Cl(g)), pyrophoric (e.g. BH3), acutely toxic (e.g. COCl2, C2N2 and PH3), greenhouse gases (e.g. CH4, SF6 and CO2) as well as the special group of interest classified as indirect greenhouse gases which includes SO2 [1–4]. Evidently, there are several undesirable and sometimes pernicious effects and conditions attributable to the excessive buildup of harmful gases in the ecosystem [5]. Such effects have overtime proven to be globally disadvantageous as they negatively impact aquatic life, reduce crop yield and expedite the development of several health conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) [6,7]. All these and many more are the major factors driving the global and multi-disciplinary collaborations to develop highly efficient, affordable, readily available and durable gas sensors to detect the presence of these toxic gases thereby preempting further damage to the ecosystem. Fundamentally, gas nanosensors are able to sense gases in the environment by measuring physical quantities in the ambience and converting them to analyzable signals [8–10]. Presently, the use of gas nanosensors now find application beyond the basic environmental pollution application to several other areas including medical, agricultural and industrial applications [11,12].

Outside the use of theoretical techniques and approaches, experimental approaches such as the use of flame emission spectrometry and UV absorption techniques have been applied towards the detection of SO2 [13–15]. On the other hand, many research works have explored the of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials such as graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) and quantum dots as gas sensors [76–79], using theoretical approaches such as the density functional theory (DFT), owing to their comparative sensing efficiency in terms of recovery cum response time, selectivity, sensitivity as well as stability and a high surface-to-volume ratio. For example, Yong et al. studied the potential of B6N6H6 monolayer as sensor for SO2 as well as eight other gases including NH3, H2, N2, CH4, H2O, N2O, CO, CO2 and HCN and used different parameters including electronic properties investigation, adsorption and sensor parameters, electron localization function (ELF) as well as other first-principles calculations to show that the B6N6H6 monolayer was better suited for sensing ammonia (NH3) and sulfur dioxide (SO2) [16] Furthermore, another DFT study aimed at investigating the gas sensing properties of pristine and Ni3 cluster doped arsenene for the detection of three gases notably, CO, NO and SO2 carried out by Wu et al. and clearly showed that whereas the pristine arsenene was preferentially suitable for sensing SO2, the Ni3 cluster doped arsenene was found to be a promising efficient single cluster adsorbent (SCA) for the detection and adsorption of the three gases considered [17]. Additionally, the 2022 research by Wenjun Zhang and co-researchers tested the gas sensing resourcefulness of C3N2 monolayers using 17 different gases including AsH3, PH3, H2S, NH3, CO2 and SO2. The overall result of the geometry optimization, chemical energetics evaluation, adsorbent-adsorbate charge transfer investigation as well as an analysis of the electronic and sensing parameters of the complexes showed that the carbon nitride, C3N2 nanosensor is most intrinsically suitable for detecting SO2 at a temperature range of 350–400 K [18]. Also, worthy of note is the study conducted by Roohi et al. using gallium nitride (GaN) nanosheets to investigate their gas sensing resourcefulness for two notable toxic gases: SO2 and NO2, using DFT-D approach at the PBE-D/DNP level of theory. The novelty of the study was further enhanced by the transition metal-doping of the GaN nanosheet using Fe, Ni and Zn. The study showed among other things that the metal functionalization of the GaN nanosheets increased their sensing potential and that the most efficient metal-functionalization was achieved with the Fe-doped GaN nanosheet which was equally found to be suitable for detecting both NO2 and SO2 gases [19] On the other hand, a recent study carried out by Chen et al. explored the gas sensing potential of a pompon-like WS2 nanostructure-based gas sensor, a nanosensor classified as transition metal dichalcogenide (TMDC). The work remarkably went beyond the DFT-based theoretical evaluation to experimental validation of the DFT studies to show that the WS2 nanostructure-based gas sensor was suitable for detecting the products of the decomposition of sulfur hexafluoride, SF6 [20].

It is important to note at this point that just very few research have been carried out to investigate the gas sensing resourcefulness of metal-functionalized Si-doped graphene quantum dots such as the 2022 study by Karbakhshzadeh et al. using Cu-functionalized Si-doped graphene quantum dots for the detection of bisulfite [21] This observation apparently indicates that this inherent potential of graphene quantum dots has not been fully explored by research compared to other nanomaterials such as nanosheets [22–25] and nanotubes [26–28]. Foundational to the aim of this research is the desideratum to theoretically investigate the gas sensing potential of transition metal functionalized silicon-doped quantum dots for the detection and adsorptive elimination of SO2 using the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory. Consequently, the Si-doped graphene quantum dot was decorated with three Group XIII transition metals: Cu, Ag and Au and then interacted with the adsorbate, SO2 at both S and O sites for each metal-functionalized Si-doped quantum dot giving six adsorbent-adsorbate systems in addition to the direct interaction of the Si-doped quantum dot with the SO2 giving a total of eight adsorbent-adsorbate complexes, the aim of the direct interactions being to serve as a benchmark to compare the effect of the metal-functionalization on the sensing potency of the Si-doped graphene quantum dots as has been repeatedly observed in several independent similar works [29–33]. The eight gas-sensor systems are designated thus: SO2_O_Si@QD (C1), SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD (C2), SO2_O_Au_Si@QD (C3), SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD (C4), SO2_S_Si@QD (C5), SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD (C6), SO2_S_Au_Si@QD (C7) and SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD (C8). To further investigate the sensing resourcefulness of the different configurations of the metal-functionalized Si-doped quantum dots first principle DFT calculations were performed at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory and was further used to investigate the electronic properties of the complexes using the HOMO-LUMO analysis grounded in the Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) theory. Other electronic properties namely: the Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) analysis, density of state (DOS) as well as the global quantum reactivity descriptors based on Koopman's approximations were equally investigated. The structural geometric parameters of the complexes were equally studied to understand the bond length changes across the eight systems. The nature of the adsorptive mechanism of the gas on the adsorbent was equally evaluated by considering their adsorption energies which was further validated using the Counterpoise Correction in other to account for the basis set supposition error (BSSE). The energetics of the systems was also studied with special interest in entropy, enthalpy and Gibb's free energy changes across the eight systems in other to gain insight into the thermodynamic nature of the adsorbent-adsorbate systems. Furthermore, the topology of the systems was evaluated using the Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) which was complemented with the Non-Covalent Interaction (NCI) analysis to fully understand the nature of the interactions between the interacting atoms.

Structural optimization to the lowest minima was performed in Gaussian16 software [34] using DFT computations at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP level of theory, a combination of the exchange-correlation functional of Lee-Yang-Parr with the dispersion correction (D3(BJ)) along with the def2-SVP basis set. This combination is suitable for studying metal functionalized quantum dots in their applications as gas nanosensors for two major reasons. Firstly, the B3LYP is a hybrid functional with correlation function making it accurate for the prediction of the structural and energetic properties [80]. Secondly, the D3(BJ) is able to correctly account for long-range van der Waals interactions thereby enhancing the investigation of the non-covalent interactions (NCI) between the gas and the surface [81]. The optimized structures were visualized using GaussView 6.0.16 [35]. The HOMO-LUMO energies were obtained using GaussView 6.0.16 and visualized on Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) software [36] while the calculation of the electronic properties and quantum descriptors was based on Koopman's approximations using equations (1)–(8) [37,38].

On interaction with SO2 at the O site, C1—C4 were formed with the number of atoms increasing to 40 in C2, C3 and C4 and 39 in C1 due to the absence of a metal on the surface and overall resulting in an increase in the number of electrons of the systems to 243, 275, 225 and 196 respectively. Similarly, in the interaction at the S site the number of atoms and electrons of C5—C8 was the same as in the case of C1—C4 as the difference was basically in configuration and not essentially the constituent structural components. It was observed that such changes in the site of interaction, i.e., the ‘O’ site (for C1 to C4) and ‘S’ site (for C5 to C8) respectively resulted in differences in the bond lengths at the gas-surface interphase as well as the charges in the atoms. For example, the bond length of the isolated SO2 was 1.464 Å for the two S=O bonds and the sum of the Mulliken charges, QMUL was zero (0). However, on adsorption with the surfaces, the differences in the nature and configuration of the adsorbate-adsorbent systems resulted in varying magnitudes of charge transferred with net charge magnitude of −0.169, −0.564, −0.648 and −0.534 e for C1, C2, C3 and C4 respectively while C5, C6, C7 and C8 had net charge magnitudes of 0.101, −0.405, −0.241 and −0.224 e respectively.

In Si@QD, the Si36—C24 had a bond length of 1.718 Å, the Si36—C3 and Si36—C9 had the same bond lengths of 1.711 Å and the C7—C8 had a bond length of 1.464 Å. In Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD the Si36—C24, Si36—C3 and Si36—C9 increased indicating an increase in reactivity and sensitivity due to metal functionalization with Ag, Au and Cu respectively; similar observation has been reported by other researchers [44]. In Ag_Si@QD, the Si36—C24, Si36—C3 and Si36—C9 bond lengths increased to 1.820, 1.806 and 1.806 Å respectively whereas in Au_Si@QD the post-functionalization bond lengths were correspondingly higher in the Si36—C24 bond alone with corresponding values of 1.823, 1.804 and 1.804 Å while in Cu_Si@QD the values were 1.826, 1.812 and 1.812 Å respectively. Furthermore, the metal-silicon interaction bond lengths ranged thus: 2.447 > 2.324>2.278 Å corresponding to Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD respectively. Additionally, it is worth mentioning that the C7—C8 bond in the metal functionalized Si@QD was shorter after functionalization with the lowest value observed in Ag_Si@QD with a magnitude of 1.4635 Å followed by the Au- and Cu-surfaces with the same values of 1.4637 Å in each case. Following adsorption of the gas on the surfaces at the O and S site respectively, the bond lengths under focus also underwent structural modifications. At the O interaction site, the S37—O38 and S37—O39 increased in each of the four systems from C1-C4 although with varying magnitudes of increase. In C1the two bonds increased by 0.013 and 0.214 to 1.477 and 1.678 Å; in C2 the S37—O38 bond increase was higher than in C1 with an increment value of 0.075 to a post-adsorption length of 1.534 while the S37—O39 bond equally increased by 0.136–1.600 Å. Similarly, in C3 and C4 the S37—O38 and S37—O39 also increased markedly by 0.051 and 0.148 to 1.515 Å and 1.611 Å while in C4 the increment factor was 0.090 and 0.136 respectively to increased post-adsorption bond lengths of 1.554 Å and 1.600 Å correspondingly. Additionally, the C7—C8 reduced in C1, increased in C2, C3 and C4 with values ranging thus: 1.487 > 1.485>1.482 > 1.459 corresponding to C4, C3, C2 and C1 respectively. Conversely, the Si36—C24, Si36—C3 and Si36—C9 bonds increased in C1 after the adsorption of SO2 while in C2, C3 and C4 the post-adsorption bond length magnitudes were smaller than before the adsorption of SO2. At the S interaction site, the two sulfur-oxygen bonds increased after adsorption in C5, C6, C7 and C8 while the C7—C8 bond length reduced in C5, C6 and C8 but increased in C7. Additionally, the Si36—C24, Si36—C3 and Si36—C9 bonds increased after SO2 adsorption in C5 and C8 while in C6 and C7 there was a marked decrease after adsorption of SO2.

Furthermore, the binding energy of the metal encapsulated surfaces were calculated using equation (14) in order to justify the stability of the adsorption processes. C2 had the highest Eb of −232.423 Ha, closely followed by C6 with an Eb of −232.369 Ha. The Cu encapsulated systems followed next with values of −226.449 and −226.384 Ha corresponding to C4 and C8 respectively. On the other hand, the Au encapsulated systems had the lowest Eb values of −200.335 and −200.33 Ha respectively. In each case, however, it is apparent that the ‘S’ site interactions had a correspondingly lower Eb magnitude with reference to the complementary ‘O’ site complement hence this predicts that the ‘S’ site interactions have a higher reactivity than the ‘O” site interactions which by their comparatively higher Eb values can be deductively considered as being more stable. The results of the geometric parameters of Si@QD, Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD, Cu_Si@QD and the eight post-adsorption systems designated as C1—C8 are presented in Table S1 and Table S2 of the supporting information (for the and the Si@QD, Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD, Cu_Si@QD and SO2) while Fig. 1 (a)–(c) is the pictorial illustration of the structures under study.

Table 1 presents the electronic properties of the silicon doped quantum dot (Si@QD), the transition metals (Ag, Au and Cu) functionalized Si@QD serving as the adsorbents, the SO2 gas which is the adsorbate as well as the electronic properties of the adsorbent-adsorbate interactions at the O and S sites of SO2 respectively giving rise to the eight interactions designated C1—C8 as equally presented in the table. The HOMO and LUMO energies are the basis on which the energy gap is based. Also called the band gap, the energy gap (E
gp ) is basically the difference between the valence band and the conduction band and summarily consists of the range of energy values called the forbidden region, a gap forbidden to electrons in the material such as semiconductor [82–84]. Mathematically, the Egp can be calculated using equation (1). where ELUMO denotes the energy of the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) also called the conduction band while the EHOMO denotes the energy of the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) which is also dubbed the valence band. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) graphically illustrate the EHOMO, ELUMO, Egp and the net dipole moment of the entire systems under study. Significantly, the variation in the energy gap is essential in predicting the comparative reactivity of a group of related systems such as adsorbent-adsorbate systems which differ mainly in the type of transition metal present as well as the site of gas-sensor interaction [85,86]. In keeping with the fundamental postulates of the Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) theory [45], the greater the Egp the lower the reactivity and the higher the stability while a lower Egp is indicative of a higher chemical reactivity and comparatively lower chemical stability [46]. As clearly presented in Table 1, the Egp of SO2 is greatest with a value of 5.3226 eV followed by the Si@QD with an Egp magnitude of 3.4640 eV. It is noteworthy that metal functionalization lowers the Egp thereby increasing the chemical reactivity [87] of the system in question hence the rationale behind the reduction in magnitude of the Egp of the three adsorbents from the ab initio 3.4640 eV to 2.7130, 3.1375 and 2.7892 eV respectively corresponding to Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD hence in order of predicted reactivity of the adsorbents (surfaces), the Ag-functionalized nanosurface is to be the most reactive as it has the lowest Egp and is closely followed by the Cu-functionalized nanosurface which differs from the first by only 0.0762 eV while the Au-functionalized nanosurface with an Egp of 3.3175 eV is predicted to be the least reactive and conversely, the most stable. At the O site, the post-interaction Egp is highest in the metal unfunctionalized nanosurface with an Egp of 3.2980 eV followed by the system with Cu having a value of 2.7430 eV and then the Ag before the Au-containing system with Egp magnitudes of 2.1252 and 1.8504 eV respectively thereby making the later the most reactive at the O site. On the other hand, the interaction at the S site reveals some rather startling fact which only tallies with the observations arising from the interaction at the O site in that just in the case of the O interactions, C5 had the highest Egp of 2.5307 eV hereby confirming again that the metal functionalization increased the sensing properties of the nanosurfaces by reducing the energy gaps as shown in the reduced Egp values of 1.9130 and 1.3932 eV corresponding to C6 and C8 respectively while the Egp of C7 was found to be greater than that of C5.

The results of the ionization potential (IP), electron affinity (EA), chemical softness (S), chemical hardness ( η ), chemical potential ( μ ), electrophilicity index (ω) and electronegativity (Χ) of the 13 systems under study have been tabulated in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2, several observations are evident. Firstly, metal functionalization reduced the values of IP of Si@QD from 5.415 eV to a range of 4.7–5.1 eV with Au_Si@QD having the highest value of 5.138 eV and Ag_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD having equal IP magnitudes of 4.716 eV individually. The EA of Si@QD reduced in magnitude following metal functionalization with the three metal-functionalized surfaces having EAs of 2.003, 2.000 and 1.937 eV corresponding to the Ag-, Au – and Cu-functionalized surfaces. Thirdly, the values of the chemical softness, S increased after metal functionalization with the Ag- and Cu-functionalized surfaces having approximate values and the Au-containing surface having the least value. Again, it is clear from Table 2 as well that the chemical hardness, η of the metal-functionalized surfaces were all smaller than the value of the hardness of Si@QD thereby corroborating that metal functionalization can greatly enhanced the reactivity of the adsorbents [47] The magnitudes of the chemical potential for the O site as well as the S site interactions also gives us insight into the influence of metal functionalization on the interactions in that C2-C4 had lower m values compared to C1 and in the same vein, C6-C8 had lower m values with respect to C5. This observation is significant because lower values of μ are more favorable to chemical reactivity [48].

Essentially, the density of states (DOS) function describes the number of available states in a system and is critical for determining carrier concentrations and energy distributions within a semiconductor [49]. The density of states undoubtedly provides valuable information about electronic structure because the structure of the density of states at the band edge provides the most information about a material's electronic and optical properties [50]. In investigating the opto-electronic properties of the eight nanosurfaces, six of which were metal functionalized with Ag, Au and Cu, the DOS were obtained as shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3, the total density of state curve (TDOS), the overlapping density of state (OPDOS) as well as the partial density of state (PDOS) curves of each of the constituent atoms in the interactions are all shown clearly and colorfully. Furthermore, the HOMO energies of each of the interactions is equally shown in dotted lines in order to vividly illustrate the effect of metal functionalization on the interactions.

Considering the interactions at the O site, in SO2_O_Si@QD, C atoms had the greatest contribution to the DOS in both the LUMO and HOMO regions. The same trend was observed in the interactions of SO2 with the Au and Cu functionalized nanosurfaces whereas in the SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD interaction, the H atoms peaked the greatest in the conduction band in terms of their contribution to the TDOS whereas in the valence band region, the C atoms had the greatest contribution in terms of their PDOS as shown by the blue curve. Furthermore, the HOMO energy level values decreased significantly following metal functionalization thereby indicating enhanced conductivity from the SO2_O_Si@QD with a HOMO energy level magnitude of −8.107 eV, followed by SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD with a significantly lower value of −6.106 eV whereas SO2_O_Au_Si@QD and SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD equally had lower magnitudes of −5.561 eV and −5.174 eV respectively. Similarly, the C atoms had highest PDOS contribution to the TDOS in all the four interactions at the S site of the adsorbent molecule already designated as SO2_S_Si@QD, SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_S_Au_Si@QD and SO2_S_CU_Si@QD respectively. Again, the effect of metal functionalization in enhancing the optoelectronic properties of the Si-doped quantum dots, Si@QD nanosurfaces is also observed from the decreasing magnitudes of the HOMO energy levels of the interactions with the values being −8.317 eV, −6.150 eV, −5.702 eV and −5.440 eV correspondingly.

In computational gas sensor studies, the analysis of the electrostatic potential is essential as it provides information about the electronic structure, reactivity, and properties of molecules [51] and is a useful tool for understanding and predicting the behavior of molecules at the atomic and molecular levels [52] hence finds multiple applications, in drug discovery, chemical reactivity, and quantum chemical calculations. Essentially, through ESP analysis, calculation and visualization of the molecular electrostatic potential can be obtained, which in turn provides useful information about the distribution of electric charge in a molecule in addition to predicting and analyzing a molecule's reactivity and chemical properties as it defines the electrophilic and nucleophilic regions [53]. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) shows the results of the ESP visualization. In Fig. 4(a) and (b), red and blue regions are identified as electron-rich and electron-deficient regions respectively. The nucleophilic regions are electron rich and have higher nucleophilicity compared to the electrophilic regions which have a comparatively lower charge density. As shown in Fig. 4a, the nucleophilic region is concentrated in SO2, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD while in the Si@QD and Ag_Si@QD surfaces, the nucleophilic and electrophilic regions are diffused. After adsorption of the gas on the surface, it can be observed in Fig. 4b that the adsorbate region had a higher concentration of charge thereby implying higher nucleophilicity within the SO2 region compared to the surface.

The dipole moment is a vector quantity which escribes the charge separation within a molecule along a specific direction [54]. The dipole moment describes the polarity of a chemical bond or molecule and is important in determining various chemical properties such as solubility, reactivity, and intermolecular interactions [55]. Basically, the dipole moment occurs when there is a difference in electronegativity between two atoms in a chemical bond, thereby resulting in an uneven distribution of electron density [56]. In this study, the dipole moment of the adsorbate (SO2), the four nanosurfaces (Si@QD, Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD) as well as the eight interactions (C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7 and C8) were calculated because the magnitude of a dipole moment can have a significant impact on a chemical species' reactivity, influencing factors like strength of intermolecular interactions, solubility, nucleophilicity, electrophilicity, and mobility. Table 3 summarizes the results of the dipole moment as obtained in this study. SO2 was calculated to have a higher μ compared to Si@QD while Ag_Si@QD, Au_Si@QD and Cu_Si@QD had higher μ which is clearly due to metal functionalization thus the metal-functionalization led to an increased polarity, solubility and mobility of the nanosurfaces as suggested by the increased μ magnitudes. It is also noteworthy that the interactions without a metal had the lowest μ values of 1.79 and 2.80 D corresponding to C1 and C5 respectively. The other three O site interactions varied thus: C4>C2>C3 while the other three S site interactions varied thus: C8>C6>C7. Overall, the two highest μ magnitudes in the O and S site interactions were observed in the surfaces with Cu with C8 having the single highest value of 8.22 D. This is specifically and generally significant as well within the wider context of the results obtained thus far as it corroborates other results especially in the FMO analysis and adsorption energy investigation.

In computational chemistry research, the determination of the adsorption energy of an interaction process is very crucial for understanding the adsorption process between the adsorbate and adsorbent, which could be either physisorption or chemisorption [57] thereby providing useful insight into the interaction strength of the adsorbate-adsorbent interaction [58]. Basically, adsorption energy can be defined as the difference in energy magnitude between the adsorbate (SO2) and the adsorbent before and after adsorption (interaction) of the two. In this study, the adsorption energies for the eight adsorbate-adsorbent interactions were obtained using DFT calculations at the same level of theory of optimization to lowest minima. Furthermore, the adsorption energies obtained were Counterpoise Corrected using the to account for the Basis Set Superposition Error (BSSE) and the results are presented in Table S4 of the supporting information and graphically illustrated in Fig. 5 . From Table S6 it is apparent that the O site interactions had higher adsorption energies compared to the S site interactions with the exception of C3 and C7 which had unfavorable adsorption energies before and after Counterpoise correction of magnitudes ranging from 0.66 to 0.92 eV for C3 and 0.81–1.05 eV for C7 thus their relative degrees of unfavourability increased after the BSSE had been taken into consideration. Additionally, C6 was observed to have an unfavorable interaction energy after Counterpoise Correction with an Ead + BSSE magnitude of 0.04 eV as equally shown in Table S6. This is true for both the metal-functionalized nanosurfaces interactions as well as those of the unfunctionalized nanosurface. This basically implies that the interactions at the O site were stronger than those at the S site. However, this also implies that in terms of reusability of sensor, the S site interactions are more efficient.

Essentially, Table S6 shows that C4 had the highest Ead magnitude of −2.42 eV followed by C2 and C1 with closely related Ead values of −1.72 and −1.71 eV respectively. C3 had an unfavorable Ead of 0.66 eV indicating that the Au-functionalized Si-doped quantum dots is not an efficient adsorbent for SO2 gas at the S interaction site. Furthermore, the O site interactions were observed to have comparatively lower Ead magnitudes. For example, Table S6 shows that C6 had the lowest Ead of −0.25 eV which indicates physisorption but after Counterpoise Correction, the Ead + BSSE magnitude of approximately 0.04 eV showed that the interaction was unfavorable. C5 had a lower Ead compared to C8 with Ead magnitudes of −0.57 and −0.66 eV respectively while the reverse was observed after the BSS error had been accounted for with C8 having a lower Ead + BSSE of −0.31 eV compared to C5 with an Ead + BSSE magnitude of −0.38 eV. The cases of C6 and C7 shows that overall, their adsorption was unfavorable with C6 having a positive Ead + BSSE magnitude of 0.04 eV and C7 having both positive values for Ead as well as for Ead + BSSE of magnitudes 0.81 and 1.05 eV respectively. Overall, the values of the adsorption energies after the Basis Set Superposition Error had been taken into consideration reveals that SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD (C8) had the lowest Ead + BSSE magnitude of −0.31 eV which indicates physisorption and which enhances its reusability as a nanosensor for the detection of SO2 gas with a feasible recovery time.

To account for the effect of d-orbital occupancy and charge rehybridization upon adsorption of SO2 gas, the NBO charge on the adsorption site as well the d-orbital projection of NBO Charges and polarization of the transition metal atoms were further analyzed and results are presented in Table 4 . The results show that charge distribution upon adsorption of SO2 was not uniform as the charge transfer in the case of adsorption on the O site of Si@QD was found to be higher compared to other species. In the same way the charge transfer upon adsorption on the S site of Si@QD was equally higher and therefore suggest that charge transfer occurred between the silicon atoms and the adsorbate mainly. The charge transfer between the adsorbate and transition metals was found to be less at both adsorption sites of the material. To further comprehend exactly the nature of adsorption of the studied gas, the effect of d-orbital projection and occupancy of each transition metal was considered. The results showed that a considerable increase in d-orbital occupancy occurred for every transition metal after adsorption. The d-orbital for Cu was found to increase from 0.9216 to 0.9765, in the case of Ag it was found to slightly reduce from 0.99988 to 0.99850. for Au, the d-orbital occupancy was found to be 0.98698 and 0.99896 before and after adsorption of SO2 at the O site. The increase in d-orbital occupancy could be due to an increase in t2g orbital occupancy. While the cases where the d orbital occupancy decrease can be tied to the minority eg set orbital occupancy which shows a reduction in the polarization of the transition metals after adsorption of SO2.

The theoretical investigation of the thermodynamics of the eight adsorbate-adsorbent interactions was carried out in order to gain further insight into how the adsorption of the SO2 on the nanosurfaces would affect the nature with respect to thermodynamic functions namely change in Gibb's free energy, enthalpy change, thermal energy, heat capacity and entropy. The results of this thermodynamic investigation are tabulated in Table 4 and Table S4 of the supporting information. Essentially, thermodynamic spontaneity is predicated by negative ΔG and ΔH values thereby implying a that such a chemical process such as an adsorbate-adsorbent adsorption process or interaction is favorable [59]. From the values obtained from the thermodynamic calculations for the eight interactions, it can be deduced that the adsorption process for all the eight interactions is thermodynamically favorable and spontaneous as well although with varying degrees of spontaneity.

The ΔG varied thus C4>C2>C1>C8>C5>C6>C3>C7 with −200.24 kcal/mol and −129.01 kcal/mol at the extremes. Similarly, the ΔH values was highest in c4 with a magnitude of −229.73 kcal/mol followed by C2 and C1 with close values of −213.54 and −212.10 kcal/mol respectively, while C8 had −188.31 kcal/mol, a value closely trailed by C5 with a ΔH value of −185.26 kcal/mol. Furthermore, C6>C3>C7 with values of −178.78, −158.54 and −154.37 kcal/mol respectively. The variation in the entropy values equally corroborate the fact that the degree of spontaneity differed across the eight interactions as the entropy values ranged thus: C7>C3>C6>C8>C2>C4>C5>C1 the value ranging from 133.45 to 166.04 Cal/mol K−1. Additionally, the values of the thermal energy, E varied very slightly from 189.26 to 190.61 kcal/mol from C5 to C6 which had the lowest and highest values respectively. Also, C1 was found to have the lowest heat capacity of magnitude 78.99 Cal/mol K−1 followed by C5 with Cv magnitude of 80.15 Cal/mol K−1 while the other six interactions were within the 86.21 to 86.62 Cal/mol K−1 range.

NBO analysis is one of the quantum chemical methods which can be used to analyze chemical bonding and investigate the extent of charge transfer between Lewis-type donor orbitals and non-Lewis acceptor orbitals in molecular systems [60]. In addition to understanding the nature of chemical bonding within a chemical system, NBO analysis also helps provide useful insight into the reactivity, stability and electronic properties of molecules [61]. Accordingly, the results of the NBO analysis is presented in Table S5 of the supporting information and show the donor atoms, acceptor species, donor and acceptor occupancies as well as the Second Order Perturbation Energy, E(2) which can equally be termed the stabilization energy. Essentially, the most important atomic transitions which contributed to the charge transfer and invariably the stability of the adsorbate-adsorbent interactions are equally shown for each of the systems considered. It is noteworthy that the most frequent transitions in this study include LP(1)
→
LP* and LP(1)
→
BD* in Si@QD; LP(1)
→
LP* in Ag_Si@QD; LP(1)
→
BD* and BD(2)
→
LP(1)* in Au_Si@QD; BD(2)*
→
LP(1)* etc. where BD(2) denote π bond and BD denote σ bond.

In the nanosurfaces, the Au-functionalized Si@QD had the lowest values of E(2) equal to 35.84 kcal/mol and due to the LP(1)
→
BD(2)* transition with the C8 as the donor and C9—C29 as the acceptor. The highest mean stabilization energy was due observed in the Ag-functionalize Si@QD and then the Cu_Si@QD before the Si@QD. The NBO analysis showed that Cu_Si@QD had the highest mean E(2) value followed by Ag_Si@QD and then Si@QD while Au_Si@QD had the lowest mean E(2). After interaction with SO2, C1 and C5 which had no metal equally had very high mean stabilization energies compared to C2, C4 and C7. C6, however had the single highest E(2) of 1157.36 kcal/mol and was closely followed by C8 with a 1153.93 kcal/mol due to LP(1)
→
LP(1) transition from C8 to C10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Advances in the density functional theory as first advanced by Kohn-Shan has revealed in greater measures, the importance of the electron density and its relationship to unravelling some significant details about adsorbent-adsorbate interactions [62]. It is on this premise that the analysis of the topology of the eight interactions was carried out using the Bader's QTAIM approach [63]. Essentially, QTAIM examines the topology of the electron density of a given molecule to determine the system's critical points. The critical points of the molecule are represented by the roots of the first order derivatives of the electron density [64]. QTAIM analysis relies on several parameters such as density of alpha and beta electrons, spin density of electrons, local information entropy, reduced density gradient, average local ionization energy, and a slew of others which are predicated on bond critical point (BCP) determination, which in turn depends on the nature of the electron density of the species under consideration [88,89]. In this study, the results of the QTAIM are presented in Table 5 and pictorially presented in Fig. 6 . Table 5 shows the different QTAIM parameters considered in this study namely: density of all electrons ( ρ(r)), Laplacian of electron density (∇
2
ρ(r)), Lagragian kinetic energy (G), Hamiltonian kinetic energy (K), potential energy density (V), energy density (H) Eigenvalues of Hessian ( λ
1
, λ
2
and λ
3
), ellipticity of electron density ( ε ), electron localization function (ELF), localized orbital locator (LOL), |⎣
1
/⎣
3
| and G(r)/|V|(r). Basically, the values of the QTAIM parameters specified above have a relationship to the electrophilicity, nucleophilicity or stability of a bond. While V
2
p > 0 is suggestive of nucleophilicity or local charge concentration while V
2
p < 0 is indicative of electrophilicity or local charge depletion [65], V2p(r) and H(r) are <0, indicates covalent (shared) interaction; V2p(r) and H(r) >0 indicates non-covalent (closed shell) while V2p(r) >0 & H(r) <0 signifies partial covalent character [66]. Additionally, G(r)/|V|(r) since values > 1 indicates non-covalency of interaction [29] while the ellipticity, ε helps us understand the anisotropic nature of the electron density such that ε values > 1 indicate bond instability [67]. The covalent or non-covalent nature of a bond can be understood from the ELF value as ELF <0.5 in typical of a non-covalent interaction while magnitudes >0.5 indicate that the interaction is covalent and in the same vein, values between 0.5 and 1 indicate electron density delocalization. Also on the electron density delocalization, LOL values < 0.5 show delocalization of electron density whereas interactions with LOL >0.5 is indicative of electron density localization. Additionally, the ratio of the first and third Eigenvalues of Hessian, |⎣1/⎣3| also provides insight into the strength of an interaction where magnitude of the quotient is directly proportional to the strength of the bond [90].

A close observation of the values in Table 5 shows that the Hamiltonian K.E., K is the additive inverse of the energy density, H . From the V
2
p values it can be observed that all the BCPs observed indicate local charge concentration. This is corroborated by the observation from the LOL which shows that all the BCPs considered have magnitudes which indicate charge density localization with the exception of Si36-O39 of SO2_O_Si@QD which of 0.4215 which is less than 0.5 thereby indicating local charge delocalization. The values of the V2p(r) & H(r) taken together reveals that each of the two BCPs considered for each of the eight interactions is partially covalent in nature. This is important for a gas sensor because the reusability is linked to the strength of the interaction between the surface and the gas. This observation is complemented to a great extent by the values of the ELF and G(r)/|V|(r) . In the case of the ELE values, however, it can be observed that the two BCPs considered for SO2_O_Si@QD corresponding to Si36-O39 and S37-C28 showed covalent character with both values greater than 0.5 i.e., 0.6740 and 0.6988 respectively. This is equally the case with Si36-O40 of SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD with an ELF magnitude of 0.5764. A similar trend is observed in Si36-O40 of SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD with an ELF value of 0.6102. Other BCPs considered are clearly non-covalent in nature as can be clearly discerned from their ELF values which are all <0.5. Furthermore, with the exception of the O38-C28 of SO2_S_Si@QD whose G(r)/|V|(r) is > 1 hence non-covalent with a magnitude of 1.3134, all the other BCPs considered have G(r)/|V|(r) values between 0.5 and 1 hence are equally shown to be non-covalent as well.

Pivotal to an understanding and thorough analysis of the interaction between an adsorbate and adsorbent is the investigation of the non-covalent interactions (NCI), which are weak interactions that are important in computational gas sensor research because they govern the adsorption and recognition processes that take place at the gas-sensor interface [68] and are essential to the understanding of the strength. Non-covalent interactions can be defined as weak forces that arise from the interaction of molecular or atomic entities due to differences in charge distribution or electronegativity rather than the sharing of electrons between atoms [69] and in a computational gas sensor study, examples of NCI include H-bonding, van der Waals interactions and electrostatic interactions. The NCI analysis of the eight interactions under study are presented in Fig. 7 . It is also worthy of note that the reduced density gradient (RDG) was employed in the analysis of the NCI. The reduced density gradient has been widely used in quantum chemical calculations to analyze chemical bond properties such as bond critical points (BCPs) and bond paths, which provide information about the nature of chemical bonding such as covalent, hydrogen, and van der Waals bonds. The RDG can also be used to investigate other electron density-related phenomena such as non-covalent interactions, charge transfer, and aromaticity [70]. As shown in Fig. 7, the various colors signify different types of types of non-covalent interaction where the blue signify strong NCI such as Hydrogen-bond and Halogen-bond; the green regions are indicative of Van der Waals interactions while the red regions signal the occurrence of repulsive interactions such as steric hindrance [71]. From the results presented in Fig. 7, the O interactions were less favorable than the S site interactions as shown by their lower magnitude of green spikes compared to the S site atoms. Furthermore, C1 had only steric and strong attractions while C2-C4 had van der Waals forces too in addition to the other two. Similarly, Fig. 7 showed that the C5 had only van der Waals interaction at the gas-sensor surface while the rest of C6-C8 had a mix of the three kinds of interaction considered in this NCI analysis.

The conductivity of semiconductors is essential to their efficacy as sensors for the detection of gases because conductivity, as opposed to resistivity is a measure of the ease of flow of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band [72]. This free flow of electrons with minimal electrostatic repulsion or resistance is pivotal the sensing efficacy of a gas sensor and can be related to the transition state equation
(12)

)
Where σ denotes electrical conductivity, T represents temperature, K denoted Boltzmann constant while A is the Richardson's constant [73]. Based on equation (12) the linear relationship between
σ
a n d
Egp becomes more pronounced such that adsorbate-adsorbent interactions with higher energy gaps will have comparatively lower
σ
than gas-sensor interactions with lower Egp values. As already established in Table 1, the variation of the Egp was seen thus: C1>C7>C4>C5>C2>C6>C3>C8 hence the interaction with the highest conductivity is the SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD with an Egp of 1.393 eV. This observation is in tandem with the results from the adsorption energy studies as well as the dipole moment analysis.

The duration required for a sensor to return to its original state and resume accurate and reliable measurements after being subjected to a disturbance or event that caused a change in its output is referred to as recovery time in the context of sensor mechanisms [74]. The recovery time is determined by several factors, including the type of sensor, the magnitude of the disturbance, the operating conditions, and the design and characteristics of the sensor. In sensor applications where rapid response and accuracy are critical, such as process control, environmental monitoring, and medical applications, recovery time is an important parameter to consider [75] hence in this study, the recovery time of the interactions were determined using equation (13).
(13)
τ =
V 0
− 1
e
− E
a d
/
K T
Where K denotes the Boltzmann constant (∼ 2.0 × 10−3 kcal/mol.K), T is the temperature and V0 represents the attempt frequency while Ead represents the adsorption energy which in this case is the BSSE corrected adsorption energy values. Again, the SO2 sensing efficacy of C8 is corroborated as it has the lowest Ead + BSSE magnitude of −0.31 eV thereby making it to have the least and most feasible recovery time.

This investigation focused on the development of a suitable material-based on metals (Ag, Au, Cu) encapsulated Si-doped quantum dots for the efficient sensing and trapping of SO2 withing the framework of first-principles density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP computational method. Eight (8) possible adsorption configurations: SO2_O_Si@QD, SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_O_Au_Si@QD, SO2_O_Cu_Si@QD, SO2_S_Si@QD, SO2_S_Ag_Si@QD, SO2_S_Au_Si@QD, and SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD were considered based on SO2 interactions with the studied materials at the -S and -O sites of the SO2 molecule. The HOMO-LUMO and quantum descriptors analyses make it evident that the order of Egp from highest to lowest for the eight adsorbent-adsorbate systems are: C1>C7>C4>C5>C2>C6>C3>C8 thereby indicating that the Cu-functionalized nanosurface with the lowest Egp of 1.3932 eV is predicted to be the most chemically reactive while the density of state plot showed that carbon had the greatest PDOS contribution to the TDOS in the eight interactions except in SO2_O_Ag_Si@QD where H atoms had a higher peak in the valence band region. Furthermore, the HOMO energy values decreased significantly in the metal functionalized nanosurfaces at both the O and S interactions thus indicating higher nanosensing potency. Furthermore, from the ESP analysis it can be seen that the charge transfer was from the surface to the gas as shown in the concentration of charge within the adsorbate region. The adsorption studies showed that for the five favorable interactions, the values of their adsorption energies before Counterpoise Correction, Ead were observed to decrease favorably after BSSE had been accounted for O site interactions: C1, C2 and C4; as well as the S site interactions: C5 and C8. Conversely, the Ead + BSSE of C3, C6 and C7 were higher than their corresponding Ead magnitudes. Overall, the values of the adsorption energies after the Basis Set Superposition Error had been taken into consideration reveals that SO2_S_Cu_Si@QD (C8) had the lowest Ead + BSSE magnitude of −0.31 eV which indicates physisorption and which enhances its reusability as a nanosensor for the detection of SO2 gas with a feasible recovery time. Furthermore, the adsorption process was observed to be thermodynamically favorable as all the eight interactions had negative DG and DH values as well as positive DS values. The DG varied thus: C4>C2>C1>C8>C5>C6>C3>C7 with −200.24 kcal/mol and −129.01 kcal/mol at the extremes. Additionally, the variation in the entropy values equally corroborate the fact that the degree of spontaneity differed across the eight interactions as the entropy values ranged thus: C7>C3>C6>C8>C2>C4>C5>C1. Similarly, the highest and lowest value of DH, E and Cv all indicate that the systems have varying degrees of thermodynamic favorability. Moreover, the NBO analysis showed that Cu_Si@QD had the highest mean E(2) value followed by Ag_Si@QD and then Si@QD while Au_Si@QD had the lowest mean E(2). After interaction with SO2, C1 and C5 which had no metal equally had very high mean stabilization energies compared to C2, C4 and C7. C6, however had the single highest E(2) of 1157.36 kcal/mol and was closely followed by C8 with a 1153.93 kcal/mol due to LP(1)
→
LP(1) transition from C8 to C10. The nature of the chemical bond formed at the gas-sensor interphase as revealed by the magnitudes of V
2
p(r) & H(r), ELF and G(r)/|V|(r) show that the interactions at the BCPs considered are mostly partially covalent in nature thereby enhancing the reusability of the nanosurfaces as gas sensors. Furthermore, the bond ellipticity, ε values for all the BCPs considered were <1 with no exception thereby indicating a favorable interaction strength which is essential to enhance the efficiency of the nanosurfaces under consideration as gas sensors for the detection of SO2. This was complemented by the NCI analysis which showed the occurrence of van der Waals forces in addition to strong interaction. The magnitude of the steric hindrance was less pronounced in the S site interactions compared to the O site interactions. Finally, C8 is predicted to have the highest conductivity and the most feasible recovery time thereby making Cu_Si@QD the best adsorbent for the adsorption of SO2 with the most theoretically predicted site of interaction being the S site. These results are in consonance with the conclusion that the metal functionalized Si-doped QDs have potential real-world device applications.
